concepts.lesson: Electric current is the flow of electric charges through a conductor. It occurs when charges move due to a potential difference across a circuit.
concepts.real_example: When a battery is connected to a bulb through wires, electrons flow through the circuit and the bulb glows.
key_points: Current is the flow of electric charge
Requires a closed circuit to flow

concepts.lesson: Potential difference is the work done to move a unit charge from one point to another in an electric circuit. It is the driving force that causes current to flow.
concepts.real_example: A battery creates a potential difference that pushes electrons through wires to light a bulb.

concepts.lesson: Ohm’s law states that the electric current through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across its ends, provided the temperature remains constant.
concepts.real_example: If the voltage applied across a resistor is doubled, the current through it also doubles, assuming resistance remains constant.
key_points: Relationship between voltage, current, and resistance
Valid only when temperature is constant

concepts.lesson: Resistance is the property of a conductor that opposes the flow of electric current. It depends on the length, cross-sectional area, and nature of the material.
concepts.real_example: Thin wires offer more resistance than thick wires made of the same material.
key_points: Depends on length, area, and material

concepts.lesson: In a series circuit, resistors are connected end-to-end so that the same current flows through each resistor. The total resistance is the sum of individual resistances.
concepts.real_example: Decorative lights connected one after another in a line behave like resistors in series.
key_points: Same current flows through all resistors

concepts.lesson: In a parallel circuit, resistors are connected across the same two points. Each resistor has the same potential difference, and the total resistance decreases.
concepts.real_example: Household wiring uses parallel circuits so that appliances receive the same voltage.

concepts.lesson: When electric current passes through a conductor, heat is produced due to resistance. This phenomenon is known as the heating effect of electric current.
concepts.real_example: Electric heaters and electric irons use the heating effect of current to produce heat.

concepts.lesson: Electric power is the rate at which electrical energy is consumed or converted in an electric circuit. It indicates how fast energy is used by an appliance.
concepts.real_example: A 100 W bulb consumes electrical energy faster than a 60 W bulb.

concepts.lesson: Electrical energy consumed by an appliance depends on its power and the time for which it is used. It is commonly measured in kilowatt-hour (kWh), also called a unit of electricity.
concepts.real_example: If a 1 kW heater runs for 1 hour, it consumes 1 kWh of electrical energy.

concepts.lesson: Safety devices such as fuses and circuit breakers protect electrical circuits from excessive current and prevent damage to appliances.
concepts.real_example: A fuse melts and breaks the circuit when too much current flows through it.
key_points: Fuse breaks circuit when current exceeds limit
